title: Waterfall Framed Grand Console Table
summary: A big pine console with mitered corners that carry the top's line straight down the sides — the 'waterfall' look — over two open shelf bays.
description:
A waterfall edge is really just a 45° miter, but a demanding one: both ends of each long board are beveled and the two ends are NOT parallel to each other, so the board comes out a long parallelogram instead of a rectangle. That's what lets the top, sides, and face frame all meet at the corner and read as one continuous surface wrapping around it, instead of a tabletop that just sits on a box.

Build the carcass first — top, two sides, bottom shelf, all joined at those mitered corners with glue and brad nails — then wrap it in a mitered 1x3 face frame front and back. The face frame is what actually holds the miters tight long-term; end-grain glue joints alone are weak, so the frame locks the whole thing in compression. Assemble the face frame as its own flat unit before nailing it to the carcass — square corners on a bench are much easier to get right than square corners fighting gravity and clamps on an assembled box.

Two center dividers split the lower half into two open bays, each with its own small mitered face frame. Keep the divider spacing exact — a 1x3's width, so the center trim sits flush when it goes on last.
timeLabel: 6–9 hrs — most of it is fitting and gluing the mitered corners cleanly

tools.note: Every long board gets a 45° bevel on non-parallel ends — a miter saw with a fence you trust is what keeps 84" cuts consistent.
Pocket-hole the face frame's mitered corners before assembly so they can't creep apart over time.
Long clamps across the miters while the glue sets make a real difference in how tight the corner ends up.

materials.name: Pine, 1x12, 10 ft
materials.note: Top, sides, bottom shelf, and dividers — pick straight boards with minimal cupping; a bow shows badly right at a mitered corner.

materials.name: Pine, 1x2, 4 ft
materials.note: Hidden interior blocking — scrap or furring strip is fine, it never shows.

cutList.note: Both ends beveled 45°, ends not parallel — a long parallelogram, not a rectangle. This is the waterfall piece.
One end beveled 45° to mate with the top's miter.
Two boards form each of the two vertical dividers.
Hidden bracing on the insides of the side panels.
Mitered 45° both ends, long point to long point, ends not parallel. Two for the front frame, two for the back.
Mitered to match the long rails.

steps.title: Build the two side panels
steps.body: Attach the 1x2 blocking to the insides of the two side 1x12s with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails. This blocking gives the top and bottom shelf something solid to nail into right near the mitered corner, where you don't want to be relying on end grain alone.

steps.title: Attach the top to the sides
steps.body: Glue and nail the top to the side panels at the mitered corners. Dry-fit first — with a parallelogram-cut board it's easy to flip a piece end-for-end by mistake and end up with a corner that doesn't close. Clamp across the joint while the glue sets if you can.

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Glue and nail the bottom shelf to the side panels the same way. Take diagonal measurements across the whole carcass and adjust before the glue sets — everything you build from here locks in whatever shape the box currently has.

steps.title: Build and attach the front face frame
steps.body: Assemble the four front 1x3 rails into a frame on the bench first, mitered corners tight, outside edges flush all around. If you have a pocket-hole jig, use it here on the miters — it's the difference between a face frame that stays tight and one that opens up a hairline gap in a year. Then glue and nail the whole frame to the front of the carcass at once, flush to the outside edges.

steps.title: Fill in the back
steps.body: Flip the project over. Fill in the open sides of the legs with a new 1x12 flush to the face frame, which is now on what's currently the bottom. Use a scrap piece of 1x3 as a spacer to hold the correct gap while you position the top-open side.

steps.title: Install the center dividers
steps.body: Attach the two center 1x12 divider boards. Check the gap between them with a scrap of 1x3 as you go — it needs to be exactly one 1x3 width so the center face frame trim sits flush against both faces when it goes on.

steps.title: Attach the back face frame
steps.body: Build and attach the back face frame the same way as the front — assembled flat, mitered corners tight, then nailed on as one unit.

steps.title: Fill in the center divider trim and finish nailing
steps.body: Glue and nail the short 1x3 trim pieces into the center divider gap, front and back. Go over the whole piece and nail off anything you missed while it was flat on the bench. Sand everything, fill nail holes, and finish.

images.alt: A long pine console table with mitered waterfall corners and two open shelf bays
